item.title: Time for the unhinged XL Wednesday CeleryDump for the good & bad people of Imgur
item.description:
greetings online friends
every time
i wonder what the best sauce of the rain and sauce era was. honey mustard?
yes
cheese = life
no way someone got 10 out of 10
i guess
there's a resemblance
::honks::
fascinating
sigh, they are always in california (my home state)
mmm hmm
awww
i'm a loner Dottie, a rebel
there was a while when tetris was the only game i had, for my gameboy
love me some sleeping on one small blanket, just on the floor
that's it for me... happy holidays gang
item.dc:creator: CeleryMan7

item.title: Ask and ye shall…
item.dc:creator: sleeperkid

item.title: What a journey my self-taught painting has been!
item.description: In 2019, at the early start of my self-taught painting journey, I embarked on a canvas adventure that marked the beginning of my artistic painting exploration. One of the first paintings (after years of drawing cars), a testament to my nascent skills, captured the raw essence of my early influences and the tentative strokes that defined my artistic identity. The color palette was hesitant, the composition tentative, and the subject matter, though ambitious, revealed the novice hand behind the brush. Fast forward to 2023, and a new chapter unfolded on my canvas. The growth and evolution in my technique and perspective became palpable. The strokes, once tentative, now carried a confident fluidity, creating a harmonious dance of colors and forms. The composition had matured, revealing a deeper understanding of balance and proportion. The subject matter, while still echoing my personal journey, bore the mark of experience and introspection. As I compared the two paintings side by side, a narrative of my artistic odyssey unfolded. The 2019 piece stood as a humble origin story, a testament to the courage it took to embark on this creative venture. In contrast, the 2023 masterpiece emerged as a proclamation of resilience and dedication, a visual manifesto of the countless hours spent honing my craft. The color choices in the newer painting were more intentional, reflecting a nuanced understanding of hue and saturation. Shadows and highlights played together in a sophisticated dance, casting a depth that was lacking in its predecessor. The evolution of my artistic voice resonated through every brushstroke, a silent conversation between past and present. The thematic resonance between the two paintings spoke of continuity—a thread connecting the novice brushstrokes of 2019 to the refined strokes of 2023. The subject matter, while a personal narrative, had transformed from a mere exploration of ideas to a profound commentary on growth, self-discovery, and the passage of time. In essence, these two paintings became a visual timeline, capturing not only the progression of my technical skills but also the evolution of my artistic soul. Each stroke told a story, and the juxtaposition of these two canvases encapsulated the transformative journey from a novice dreamer to a seasoned artist embracing the canvas with confidence and purpose.
item.dc:creator: realisticcarpainting

I lied. I hope you enjoyed all 26 memes

I’ve stolen many a Harry Potter meme slash funny over the last year, so I thought I would share. I’m re-reading the series again after getting a new shiny set of first editions for Christmas and I’m just so excited!! https://imgur.com/gallery/wIHq5

item.title: Streaming Services kind of suck
item.description: I use a few services but at the rate of price increases and more and more services popping up with some dying before they're a year old, the constant price raises and even ads being introduced into what was an ad free experience: this is getting a little out of hand to say the least.
item.dc:creator: lemonofdoom2

item.title: Images of Note: i use alpine
item.description:
AAAAAAAAA
owo
it is highly radioactive and makes the demon core seem like Carbon in comparison.
Arch Linux always feels like the type of shit you need to do a satanic ceremony for. LET ME USE MINT IN PEACE!
how do we enforce those traditions and values when the socio-economic system dissolves them? state and societal violence of course!
hell yeah, imagine the shitposts
:3
Hello, it's your friendly neighbourhood nerd abbiistabbii. Did you know that a lot of the Adobe Suite has open source equivalents that are free and available on Mac, Linux and Windows? It's true! Photoshop = GIMP Premiere = Kdenlive Animator = Blender (yes they have a 2D suite now) I could go on.
AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA
nobody calls it gnu+linux except neckbeards and Richard Stallman
I am both the gun giver and the goblin.
For Chats: http://discord.abbii.gay For Shitposts: http://tumblr.abbii.gay For srs and maybe shitposts: http://mastodon.abbii.gay
item.dc:creator: abbiistabbii

item.title: Found the guys Obituary...
item.description: I usually creep on my SS giftee to see if they got the gift and if they liked it. I sent the present on Dec 3rd and have heard crickets. The Guys profile has been empty for the last few months (Didn't post much to begin with), found him on FB, empty for several months. Googled the name and city and found his Obituary. Died Nov 24th I feel terrible. Heres to you mate! hope someone got use out of the stuff I sent and it didn't cause any bad feelings.
item.dc:creator: bigmuffin1

item.title: Fighting for Freedom
item.description:
The slave-ship Creole was part of the 'coastwise' US slave trade, the movement of American-born slaves from one part of the US to another by ship. The Creole was carrying 135 slaves from Richmond, Virginia, to New Orleans, Louisiana. One of the slaves onboard was Madison Washington, who had seemingly already escaped slavery to Canada some years prior and been recaptured* when he returned to the United States. On 7 November 1841 he led a group of 18 other men in an attack on the crew and passengers, with one passenger - a slave trader - being killed; they successfully took the ship, with several wounded crew members hiding in the rigging until the following morning, when they surrendered. Several would later state that during the fracas the enslaved people had shouted various slogans indicating they were going to kill all white people on the ship, but after taking control and some initially frenzied minor looting, tempers cooled and one white man, Jacob Leitner, a Prussian-born ship's cook, even had his pocketwatch returned to him with apologies. After initially considering trying to head for Liberia in West Africa - an unrealistic prospect with limited supplies - an enslaved man called Ben Blacksmith made the others aware that there had been previous cases of American slaves arriving in the British West Indies - where slavery had been abolished in 1834** - being freed upon landing***, so they decided to set sail for the Bahamas. During the voyage, the 19 maintained a tight control over the ship, keeping the other enslaved people to their regular confinement schedule, further confining some white persons aboard with the enslaved, and maintaining a constant watch at the ship's wheel. The crew were also warned that they were not to speak to one another unless one of the 19 was present - on pain of being "thrown overboard". When the Creole reached Nassau, Madison ordered every weapon onboard the ship tossed overboard except a single pistol and single musket, held by him and one other ringleader. The ship was met by the Nassau harbour pilot and his crew - all black - who told the slaves they should try to get on-shore at once, which would make any attempt to recapture the escapees by Americans a kidnapping attempt under British law. Before any of the escapees made it ashore however, the pilot took Creole's white first mate Zephaniah Gifford ashore to find medical aid for those wounded in the uprising - but before returning to the ship Mr Gifford informed the US Consul John Bacon (1789-1860) of the slave revolt. The Consulate moved quickly and lodged a complaint of piracy to the Nassau authorities under Governor Francis Cockburn (1780-1868, he was brother of Rear Admiral George Cockburn, who had released and armed American slaves during the War of 1812), leading all on board the Creole being confined to the ship, guarded by a force of twenty local British troops - all black including their Corporal and Sergeant, except for a white commissioned officer who led them, from the 2nd West India Regiment (a regiment which, when originally formed in 1796, had been filled by the British Army purchasing slaves). Word had spread of the circumstances that had brought Creole into port and several small boats of local people, armed with clubs, surrounded the ship, to protect the slaves from any reprisals and stop any attempt by the Creole to flee port. On-shore, First Mate Gifford attempted to purchase pistols and cutlasses, but was refused service, reporting that the inhabitants of Nassau, both white and black, said "there goes that damned slaver" wherever he went.
[Picture: The Creole case mentioned in Nile's National Register, Baltimore, June 11, 1842, as part of Anglo-American negotiations about compensatory payments. The Enterprise was a slave ship that was forced by bad weather into Hamilton, Jamaica, on February 11, 1835, only seven months after the end of slavery in the British Empire; seventy-two slaves onboard were freed after Bermudan former slave Richard Tucker, backed up by a Royal Navy gunboat, issued a writ of Habeus Corpus to the Captain of the Enterprise, forcing the Captain to present the slaves in a Bermudan court, where they were given the free choice to stay or return to the US. On January 22 1842, the National Register devoted two and a half pages to an exhaustive report on the Creole.] The US Consulate had been covertly organising an attempt by American sailors (from the cargo ship Congress and brig Louisa, also in Nassau) to board Creole and sail her out of Nassau to rendevous with a US Navy ship. Without access to additional weapons this was foiled when an desultory 8am approach by American sailors on 12 November (who hid their small cache of muskets by wrapping them in an American flag) of Congress was spotted and the black British soldiers gathered on the deck of the Creole, loaded and bayonets fixed, threatened to open fire on them.
[Image: Private of the 8th West Indies Regiment, in approximately the form of uniform those of the 2nd would have worn. This painting is in the collection of the National Army Museum (Chelsea, London) where, incidentally, I'll be launching my book in 2024.] Later on 12 November, the British authorities granted leave for the slaves onboard - apart from the 19 who had rose up - to leave the Creole as free people, with 50 taking up an offer of a paid-for onward journey to Jamaica on 15 November. Each enslaved person who left Creole was transported ashore in one of the many small boats that had surrounded her, with a great amount of jubilation amongst the citizens of Nassau, which must have been quite the emotional experience for those who had began their journey on Creole chained below decks. A final American protest concerned the retention of the 'personal property' of the enslaved people onboard, with Consul Bacon asserting that slaves had no personal property, being property themselves; this led to a British party going aboard Creole without American permission and removing clothes, blankets and other items from the parts of the ship that had held slaves. The 19 enslaved people who had actively captured Creole were to be tried for piracy. The 19 were held in custody for about 6 months, during which two died (one from wounds sustained during the uprising), but when their case was heard in Nassau they were found, according to the ruling of a dispatch from London regarding Maritime and British law, to have committed no crime, as persons illegally detained have the right to fight for their freedom - including the use of deadly force. "The sole object of compelling [capturing] the Creole was to take them to some Port where they might obtain their freedom, and we [the Law Officers of the Crown] think that the act of slaves committed with such intent and object does not amount to piracy."
[Image: Part of Consul Bacon's letter of protest to Governor Cockburn concerning the actions of the British authorities in relation to the enslaved people on Creole.] This not only released the surviving liberators, but also ended an attempt by the US Government to have the now 17 men transferred to New Orleans for trial. There was significant outrage in the United States over this loss of property, with the intercession of the black British troops on the American Consulate's attempt to recapture the American ships with American sailors becoming a key point of upset, suggesting that if the British authorities had simply stood apart, no slaves would have been lost to their owners. During Congressional debates on the case Alabama Senator William R King (1787-1853, briefly the 13th Vice President of the United States) grandly announced "unless the British Government should retrace her steps, war must inevitably ensue" and South Carolina Senator John C Calhoun (1782-1850, the former 7th Vice President of the United States) declared with zero irony that "if we cannot obtain justice, every man with an American heart in his bosom will be ready to raise his hand against oppression"! To these statements, abolitionist Joshua Giddings (1795-1864) of Ohio responded that it would damage the honour of the United States if the national government made an attempt to re-enslave those on the Creole, as slavery was not within the competence of the United States' Federal Government, but a state matter (turning the traditional argument for slavery as a state's rights issue on its head), for which he was censured by an outraged House of Representatives vote (125-69) - being barred from speaking in his own defence, he subsequently resigned from Congress, and was overwhelmingly re-elected (7,469-383) in a special election by the abolitionist electorate of Ohio.
[Picture: A low-quality copy of an image that purports in several online sources to be of Madison Washington. I would guess that given the bestial cast to the figure, if it is meant to be Mr Washington, it likely comes from a contemporary American publication, emphasising the piratical.] The decade-long diplomatic rift between the US and Great Britain over this and other incidences led, eventually, to a compensatory agreement in 1853 - but no individuals were returned to bondage. Sadly, five slaves - all women and children - had remained hidden on Creole, and so were returned to slavery when the Creole was released back to American sailors. Nonetheless, with 128 American slaves being freed, the mutiny on the Creole is the single most successful slave revolt in the history of the United States, barring events where enslaved African-Americans were freed by foreign military action, such as the estimated 6000 who fled to British forces in the War of 1812. In 1843, in Parliament, Viscount Palmerston asked Her Majesty's Government if, as a result of cases like the Creole, and the diplomatic problems it caused, there was any change to orders for British governors who may encounter such situations, Lord Stanley replied: "there [has been] no deviation from the instructions issued by the late Government. The instructions were, that in case any complaint was made to the British governor, upon credible testimony, that any persons in a British port were detained against their will, it was the duty set forth, to afford such persons relief and protection."
[Picture: site of Lumpkin's Jail, Richmond, Virgina, next to the I-95.] Several of the slaves on Creole were the property of Robert Lumpkin (?-1865), an up-and-coming slave trader soon to be owner of "Lumpkin's Jail", which was to be Richmond's largest slave-holding facility. Lumpkin purchased the jail in 1844, so the loss of those slaves on Creole that he owned did not cause him much financial distress. Robert Lumpkin was an interesting example of the hypocrisy of slavery, he married one of his slaves, Mary, and had five children with her. He relocated his family to Pennsylvania during the US Civil War (1861-1865) so that, in the event of his death, his wife and children would not be sold into slavery to pay off his debts, as Virginian law then allowed. In the final days of the Confederacy, he was noted for trying to flee Richmond by train with 60 chained slaves as the Union Army approached, finding no train with enough room, he abandoned them in the street to flee alone. After his death in late 1865, following the Civil War, his slave-holding facility became the property of Mary, who sold it for the foundation of a black seminary, which lives on today as Virginia Union University. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Known names of those enslaved people who rose up aboard Creole: Madison Washington, Ben Blacksmith, Elijah Morris, Doc Ruffin, Pompey Garrison, George Portlock, Tyler, Addison, T. Smallwood, William Glover, America Woodis, George Benton, Adam Carney, Reuben Knight and Jordan Philips ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- If you enjoy my writing, it may interest you to note that I've written my first book coming out in early 2024 - about the Colonial Marines, African-Americans who found freedom fighting for Britain in the War of 1812, and their post-war lives as free people in Canada, Trinidad and - sadly briefly - as the military of an emerging Afro-Native American nation in Florida. I am particularly proud that within the book, where possible, I have named every enslaved and formerly enslaved person. It is now available for pre-order. UK Amazon: https://www.amazon.co.uk/Black-Redcoats-Colonial-Marines-1814-1816/dp/1399034014 US Amazon: https://www.amazon.com/Black-Redcoats-Colonial-Marines-1814-1816/dp/1399034014/ I'm also going to plug a track by friend-of-a-friend of mine's band, "Forlorn Hope", who did this excellent track which pleases British historian me no end: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hp5IsSn8_Rg Asterix-Notes * The details of Washington's life are generally unclear, and Frederick Douglass wrote a fictional account of his life, which in some sources seems to have been taken as fact. It is possible Mr Washington returned to the US to find and free his family. **Although the legal status of "enslaved" had been abolished in the majority of the British Empire in 1836, it was in many areas followed by involuntary "apprenticeships" of differing length - with former slaves continuing to work for their former owners, albeit for pay, before actual freedom to make their own choices came. British abolition was also made possible by a huge Government-paid compensation scheme to slaveowners, a vast redistribution of British public wealth to an already privileged minority, the debt for which only cleared off Her Majesty's Treasury's books in 2015, meaning a majority of modern tax-paying black Britons descended from slaves have paid for their ancestor's freedom. ***Comet 1830, Encomium 1833, Enterprise 1835, and Hermosa 1840 were all cases where US slave-ships had came into British ports and had their human cargoes freed. -------------------------------------------------- A fine collection on the legal arguments surrounding several incidences of the freeing of American slaves by British authorities, and the arbitration of compensation: https://legal.un.org/riaa/cases/vol_xxix/26-53.pdf
item.dc:creator: BodicaRexVII

item.title: Ukrainian soldiers saying goodbye to their brother Andrii Trachuk on Independence Square, Kyiv, on December 15, 2023.
item.description: Andrii, call sign "Slon" (Elephant), was a participant in the Revolution of Dignity (2013-2014) and defended Ukraine from Russian aggression. He died on December 9, 2023 in the Kherson region. Eternal memory and eternal glory to Ukrainian Hero! Deepest condolences to everyone who knew Andrii. Source: Evgeniy Maloletka
item.dc:creator: Limpingteddybear
